verses.text: There was a man of mount Ephraim, whose name was Micah. He said unto his mother, The eleven hundred shekels of silver that were taken from thee, about which thou cursedst, and spakest of also in mine ears, behold, the silver is with me; I took it. His mother said, Blessed be thou of the LORD, my son. When he had restored the eleven hundred shekels of silver to his mother, his mother said, I had wholly dedicated the silver unto the LORD from my hand for my son, to make a graven image and a molten image: now therefore I will restore it unto thee. Yet he restored the money unto his mother; and his mother took two hundred shekels of silver, and gave them to the founder, who made thereof a graven image and a molten image: and they were in the house of Micah. Guidance: A house-by-house portrait of how broken Israel had become. The book opens this final section with a small story: a son named Micah ('who is like the LORD') steals from his mother, who has cursed the thief. Hearing her curse pronounced on himself, he confesses. She immediately rolls back the curse with 'Blessed be thou of the LORD' — quick to invoke God's name in both directions. Then the actual disaster: she dedicates the recovered silver 'unto the LORD' to make a graven image. The second commandment (Exod 20:4) is broken in the name of obedience to the first. Eleven hundred shekels — the same number each Philistine lord paid Delilah (16:5). Money that should have been the LORD's keeps building shrines for everything except the LORD.

The man Micah had an house of gods, and made an ephod, and teraphim, and consecrated one of his sons, who became his priest. Guidance: 'A house of gods' — an entire shrine in his hill-country home, complete with ephod (priestly vest), teraphim (household idols, the kind Rachel stole in Gen 31), and his own son as priest. Every element is a violation: a shrine outside the tabernacle (Deut 12), an ephod made by a non-priest (Exod 28), teraphim of any kind, a non-Levite priest. He invents his own religion using all the right vocabulary. Pious devotion plus theological ignorance produces idols indistinguishable from worship — and Micah probably thought he was a good man. Self-styled spirituality builds a 'house of gods' while believing it serves God.

In those days there was no king in Israel, but every man did that which was right in his own eyes. Guidance: The book's recurring epitaph (Judges 17:6, 18:1, 19:1, 21:25), spoken here for the first time. 'No king' is partly a political statement and partly a theological one — Israel had no human ruler and no longer obeyed the divine one. 'Every man did that which was right in his own eyes' is the textbook definition of a society in moral free-fall. Eyes had been important in this book: Samson lost his to lust; Israel lost theirs to autonomy. The cure will not come for centuries — and even then will be incomplete.

There was a young man out of Beth-lehem-judah of the family of Judah, who was a Levite, and he sojourned there. The man departed out of the city from Beth-lehem-judah to sojourn where he could find a place: and he came to mount Ephraim to the house of Micah, as he journeyed. Micah said unto him, Whence comest thou? And he said unto him, I am a Levite of Beth-lehem-judah, and I go to sojourn where I may find a place. Guidance: A Levite from Bethlehem in Judah — far from his Levitical city assignment (Levites had specific cities, Josh 21) — wandering 'to sojourn where I may find a place.' He is a religious professional with no clear calling, looking for work. The Levite class was supposed to depend on the tithe of Israel; this one is freelancing. The economic disorder reflects the spiritual disorder. When the people stop tithing, the priests start drifting; when the priests drift, they become available for hire. The next paragraph is exactly that transaction.

He is a religious professional with no clear calling, looking for work. The Levite class was supposed to depend on the tithe of Israel; this one is freelancing. The economic disorder reflects the spiritual disorder. When the people stop tithing, the priests start drifting; when the priests drift, they become available for hire. The next paragraph is exactly that transaction.

Micah said unto him, Dwell with me, and be unto me a father and a priest, and I will give thee ten shekels of silver by the year, and a suit of apparel, and thy victuals. So the Levite went in. The Levite was content to dwell with the man; and the young man was unto him as one of his sons. Guidance: A job offer: ten shekels yearly (a livable wage but hardly grand), a robe, room and board. 'Be unto me a father and a priest' — Micah wants the spiritual prestige of having a Levite, and he calls a young man 'father' to flatter him into the deal. The Levite accepts. He has just exchanged his vocational identity for a salary. Worship for hire is not new; the chapter just names it. The 'father' becomes a son ('unto him as one of his sons') — Micah is paying him as a son and addressing him as a father, an inversion that captures the chapter's confusion about every relationship.

Micah said unto him, Dwell with me, and be unto me a father and a priest, and I will give thee ten shekels of silver by the year, and a suit of apparel, and thy victuals. So the Levite went in. The Levite was content to dwell with the man; and the young man was unto him as one of his sons. Guidance: A job offer: ten shekels yearly (a livable wage but hardly grand), a robe, room and board. 'Be unto me a father and a priest' — Micah wants the spiritual prestige of having a Levite, and he calls a young man 'father' to flatter him into the deal. The Levite accepts. He has just exchanged his vocational identity for a salary. Worship for hire is not new; the chapter just names it. The 'father' becomes a son ('unto him as one of his sons') — Micah is paying him as a son and addressing him as a father, an inversion that captures the chapter's confusion about every relationship.

Micah consecrated the Levite; and the young man became his priest, and was in the house of Micah. Then said Micah, Now know I that the LORD will do me good, seeing I have a Levite to my priest. Guidance: Micah — a non-priest — 'consecrates' a Levite. A purely transactional ordination. Then the chapter's most chilling line: 'Now know I that the LORD will do me good, seeing I have a Levite to my priest.' He has built a forbidden shrine, made forbidden images, hired a freelance Levite, and concluded that this hybrid religion guarantees God's favor. Theological self-deception at its purest. The LORD's blessing cannot be triggered by collecting the right religious staff. The chapter that began by stealing money to make a god ends with a man certain that god will reward him for it. Chapter 18 will reveal what kind of 'good' actually comes.
